title: Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD)
concept: Stochastic Gradient Descent speeds up training by updating weights using just one random data point at a time.

exercises.prompt: Recap: The optimizer uses gradient descent to repeatedly adjust model weights to minimize the…?

options.text: total size of the training dataset
number of features in the model

exercises.explain: Right! The optimizer repeatedly adjusts weights to minimize the cost function, reducing overall error. Now, let's speed this up!

cards.title: Checking every house
cards.body: Imagine pricing a home. Standard gradient descent looks at every single house on the market before making a tiny correction. That's incredibly slow!

cards.title: One at a time
cards.body: Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) updates the model's weights using only one random example at a time, instead of checking the entire dataset.

cards.title: The foggy mountain
cards.body: It's like walking down a foggy mountain in the dark. Instead of scanning the entire horizon, you look down, feel the slope under one foot, and take a quick step.

cards.title: Speed vs. Precision
cards.body: If you have 1 million users, standard GD computes 1 million errors before updating weights once. SGD makes 1 million updates in the same time!

cards.title: Noise is a helper
cards.body: You might think checking one point at a time is too messy. But this noise actually helps SGD jump out of shallow, bad valleys (local minimums) that trap standard GD.

cards.title: Batch GD vs. SGD
cards.body: Comparing the two ways of descending the error hill:

left.text:
Reads all data
Slow, steady, direct path

right.text:
Reads 1 random row
Super fast, zigzag path

exercises.prompt: Why is standard Batch Gradient Descent slow on massive datasets?

options.text: It must calculate the error for every single row of data before taking a single step.
It forgets the weights after every step.
It only works on small computers.

exercises.explain: Yes! Standard Batch GD has to loop through the entire dataset to compute a single weight update, which is slow for big data.

exercises.prompt: True or false: SGD takes a zigzag path down the error hill because it only uses one random data point at a time.
exercises.explain: True! Because each random data point is slightly different, the steps bounce around, resulting in a noisy, zigzag path.

exercises.sentence: While standard Gradient Descent uses all data for a step, SGD uses just ___ random example.
exercises.explain: Exactly! Stochastic means random, and SGD evaluates exactly one random training example to make each step.

exercises.prompt: Which optimization approach updates the model's weights the fastest when working with 10 million rows?
exercises.explain: SGD updates weights immediately after checking each single row, making it dramatically faster to start learning than standard GD.

exercises.prompt: Your model training is taking hours because you have 50 million image files. You switch from standard Gradient Descent to SGD. What should you expect?

options.text: Weights will update millions of times faster, though the path to the bottom will look zigzagged and noisy.
The model will stop training entirely and throw an error.
The training will take even longer because SGD does more math per step.
The model will reach a perfect zero error in just one single step.

exercises.explain: Boom! SGD gets to work immediately, taking millions of quick, noisy steps rather than waiting hours for a single steady one. Next: how to get the best of both worlds!
